text: Henri Poincaré described his discovery of Fuchsian functions not as the product of sustained effort but of a fortuitous interruption. He had set aside a difficult problem to take a geological excursion. The moment he stepped onto a bus, the answer arrived fully formed. He argued that mathematical invention depends on an aesthetic sensibility — an intuition that filters which unconscious combinations are worth surfacing into consciousness. This pre-verbal sense of rightness is not mystical; it is the pattern-recognition machinery of a prepared mind operating below the threshold of deliberate thought. Preparation without rest, he concluded, produces accumulation without insight.
question: According to the passage, what function does Poincaré attribute to aesthetic sensibility in mathematical invention?

options.text: It replaces the need for rigorous verification.
It accelerates the incubation stage by narrowing the problem space.
It allows the prefrontal cortex to engage more deeply with abstract symbols.
It filters which unconscious combinations are worth surfacing into consciousness.
